frameworks.name: Eysenck's biological theory of personality
frameworks.one_line: links extraversion to low cortical arousal and neuroticism to limbic reactivity

frameworks.name: parametric fMRI modulation of arousal and valence
frameworks.one_line: BOLD signal regressed on trial-by-trial arousal and valence ratings

frameworks.name: attenuated reward processing hypothesis in neuroticism
frameworks.one_line: predicts reduced orbitofrontal engagement for rewarding emotional stimuli in high-N individuals

core_question: How do extraversion and neuroticism modulate brain activation evoked by the arousal and valence of emotional images?
key_assumptions: subjective arousal and valence ratings index relevant emotional dimensions
BOLD signal scales parametrically with these affective dimensions
Eysenck's reticulothalamic and limbic loci underlie personality differences
manipulations: emotional image viewing with trial-by-trial arousal ratings
key_findings: high neuroticism predicted reduced sustained orbitofrontal activation and attenuated right temporal valence response
high neuroticism predicted increased right medial prefrontal response to arousal
extraversion was negatively related to arousal response in right cerebellum
extraversion was positively related to arousal response in right insula
interpretations: results support Eysenck's biological theory of personality
high neuroticism is linked to attenuated reward-related processing
